chunks.en: I usually commit at a good stopping point, so my changes stay small and easy to follow.
chunks.ja: 私はたいてい区切りのいいところでコミットするので、変更は小さくて追いやすいままです。

chunks.en: The words I put in the message are usually "fix", "add", or "refactor" at the start.
chunks.ja: メッセージの書き出しには、たいてい「fix」「add」「refactor」といった言葉を使います。

chunks.en: My message gets too vague when I write something like "fixed various things" late at night.
chunks.ja: 私のメッセージは、夜遅くに「いろいろ修正」みたいに書いてしまうと、雑になりすぎます。

chunks.en: When I spot a typo, I just run git commit amend to fix the message quickly.
chunks.ja: タイプミスに気づいたら、git commit --amend を実行してメッセージをさっと直します。

outline: Point 1: the starter words I reuse
Wrap-up: fixing bad messages with amend

modelTalk.text: Honestly, I try to commit in small chunks, whenever I hit a good stopping point. If I wait till the end, I've usually forgotten what I even changed. So I'd rather save little bits as I go. For the wording, I mostly start with "fix" when I've squashed a bug, or "add" when there's something new. Sometimes I'll throw in "update" or "refactor" too. But when I'm tired, my messages get lazy, like "fixed some stuff." That's the worst, because two weeks later I can't tell what "stuff" means. When I catch a typo right after, I just amend it quickly. I've redone messages more times than I'd like to admit. If I've already pushed, though, I usually leave it alone. It's not worth the hassle. So my rule of thumb is simple: commit often, and write it like future-me is watching. That saves me every time.

chunks.en: To be honest, I usually commit in small chunks whenever I hit a natural stopping point.
chunks.ja: 正直なところ、区切りのいいところに来るたびに、小さくコミットすることが多いです。

chunks.en: The words I put in the message are usually things like "fix", "add", or "refactor".
chunks.ja: メッセージの書き出しに使う言葉は、たいてい「fix」「add」「refactor」といったものです。

chunks.en: My message gets too vague when I'm tired and just type something like "various fixes".
chunks.ja: 疲れているときに「いろいろ修正」みたいに書いてしまうと、メッセージが雑になりすぎます。

chunks.en: When I spot a typo, I usually fix a bad message by running git commit amend.
chunks.ja: タイポに気づいたときは、たいてい git commit --amend を使って雑なメッセージを直します。

outline: Point 1: the starter words I rely on
Wrap-up: how I fix a bad message

modelTalk.text:
To be honest, I usually commit in small chunks whenever I hit a natural stopping point. I'd rather do that than dump everything at the end of the day. That way, if something breaks, I can roll back without losing much.

As for the words, I nearly always kick off with "fix", "add", or "update". They're short and they get straight to the point, you know?

That said, sometimes I get lazy and write something vague like "various changes". When that happens, it's usually because I've bundled too much into one commit. Actually, that's a red flag telling me I should've split things up.

And typos? Oh, they sneak in all the time. If I catch one before pushing, I'll just run git commit amend and rewrite it. It's a lifesaver, really. But once it's pushed, I tend to leave it — rewriting shared history isn't worth the hassle.
